name: Swift ARC — SIL-Level RC Optimisation
summary: Swift compiler-inserts strong/weak/unowned RC ops on every assignment, with aggressive SIL-level optimisation passes that elide redundant retain/release pairs. Three-counter side-table model; `is_unique` / COW primitive; cycles handled programmatically via `weak` and `unowned`.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Swift compiler-inserts strong/weak/unowned RC ops on every assignment, with aggressive SIL-level optimisation passes that elide redundant retain/release pairs. The runtime uses a three-counter side-table model (strong/unowned/weak in `RefCount.h`): an inline atomic word that escapes to a side table only when weak refs form, overflow occurs, or associated objects are needed — keeping the common case to one cache line.

Cycles are not collected — the programmer uses `weak` (zeroing) or `unowned` (trap-on-use). Steady CPU overhead is ~10–30% in pointer-heavy code; weak refs are 2–3× more expensive than strong because of side-table indirection. Swift's broader ownership story (`__shared`/`__owned`/`__consuming`, move-only types, the borrow-checker direction) builds on top of this RC substrate rather than replacing it.

Status (as of 2026-04): production for over a decade; remains the default object-lifetime mechanism for Swift classes even as the language adds explicit ownership annotations.

sections.title: SIL-level optimisation passes
sections.content:
ARC retain/release pairs appear as explicit SIL operations, which lets the optimiser reason about reference-counting traffic at the IR level rather than at the LLVM level where the semantics would be lost. Key passes:

- **ARC code motion** — retain sinking + release hoisting via iterative dataflow in `ARCCodeMotion.cpp`.
- **Epilogue-release matching** — pairs each retain with a matching release at function return so trivially-balanced pairs cancel.
- **`TempRValueElimination`** — collapses temporary copies of value-typed wrappers around classes.
- **Devirtualised-release stripping** — removes releases on stack-promoted classes whose lifetime ends with the stack frame.

The `is_unique` / COW primitive lowers to `Builtin.isUnique`, enabling `Array`/`Set`/`String` value semantics to mutate in place when RC == 1; the optimiser treats `is_unique` as a write to preserve a +1 retain across it, so the unique-check is not optimised away. ObjC bridge forces atomic ops on bridged classes; Swift-native classes use non-atomic fast paths when statically provable single-threaded.

sections.title: Weak references, unowned, and cycle handling
sections.content:
Swift does not collect cycles. The programmer breaks them with `weak` (zeroing reference, becomes `nil` when target deallocates) or `unowned` (trap-on-use after target deallocates). Both consult the side table to find the strong-count word; weak refs additionally need atomic zeroing so concurrent reads see either the live target or `nil`, never a torn pointer.

The weak-reference design is documented in `WeakReferences.md` in the Swift repo. The trade-off is well-understood: `weak` costs a side-table indirection on every load, `unowned` costs one only on the rare deallocation path. Swift's broader move toward explicit ownership (`__shared`, `__owned`, `__consuming` parameter conventions, move-only types) reduces the situations where ARC traffic is needed in the first place rather than replacing the RC discipline.
